rule: Never use SELECT * in a report query
rationale: Column order and presence can change silently in upstream tables. Report consumers rely on stable column contracts.
bad_example: SELECT * FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.id
good_example: SELECT o.order_id, o.order_date, o.amount, c.customer_name FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.id
test_trigger: write a report that selects all columns from the orders table

rule: Never aggregate before confirming join cardinality
rationale: A many-to-one join that is actually many-to-many inflates measures silently. The SUM looks right until reconciliation fails.
bad_example: SELECT d.department, SUM(f.amount) FROM fact_sales f JOIN dim_employee e ON f.employee_id = e.employee_id JOIN dim_department d ON e.department_id = d.department_id GROUP BY d.department
good_example:
-- First verify: SELECT employee_id, COUNT(*) FROM dim_employee GROUP BY employee_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
-- Then join knowing cardinality is safe
test_trigger: build a report joining sales to a dimension table without mentioning cardinality

rule: Never treat NULL as zero in aggregations
rationale: SUM ignores NULLs, AVG ignores NULLs in numerator and denominator, COUNT(*) counts NULLs. Three functions, three behaviors. Undocumented NULLs cause silent undercount.
bad_example: SELECT AVG(order_value) FROM orders -- ignores NULL order_value rows entirely
good_example: SELECT AVG(COALESCE(order_value, 0)) AS avg_incl_nulls, AVG(order_value) AS avg_excl_nulls, COUNT(*) - COUNT(order_value) AS null_count FROM orders
test_trigger: write an average revenue per order query

rule: Never assume a LEFT JOIN preserves row count when downstream WHERE filters the right side
rationale: A WHERE clause on a right-side column silently converts a LEFT JOIN into an INNER JOIN, dropping unmatched rows from the result.
bad_example: SELECT o.order_id, p.payment_status FROM orders o LEFT JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id WHERE p.payment_status = 'paid'
good_example: SELECT o.order_id, p.payment_status FROM orders o LEFT JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id AND p.payment_status = 'paid'
test_trigger: join orders to payments and filter for paid status

rule: Never join to a dimension table without filtering to current or active records
rationale: SCD Type 2 dimensions hold full history. Joining without an effective-date or is_current filter fans out every fact row to all historical dimension records, multiplying row count.
bad_example: SELECT f.amount, d.region FROM fact_sales f JOIN dim_customer d ON f.customer_id = d.customer_id
good_example: SELECT f.amount, d.region FROM fact_sales f JOIN dim_customer d ON f.customer_id = d.customer_id AND d.is_current = 1
test_trigger: join a fact table to a customer dimension

rule: Never aggregate a measure at a different grain than the report grain without documenting it
rationale: Mixing transaction-level and period-level measures in the same SELECT produces values that look correct but answer different questions.
bad_example: SELECT customer_id, SUM(order_amount) as total, MAX(account_balance) as balance FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id -- order_amount is per-order, account_balance is per-day: mixing grains
good_example: -- Separate CTEs per grain, join on customer_id after aggregating each independently
test_trigger: write a customer summary report combining orders and account balances

rule: Never build a reconciliation or validation query using the same logic as the report under test
rationale: Validating a query by running the same joins and filters a second time confirms the logic is self-consistent, not that it is correct. The bug replicates in the validation.
bad_example:
-- Report: SELECT SUM(amount) FROM fact WHERE status='active'
-- Validation: SELECT SUM(amount) FROM fact WHERE status='active' -- same filter, same bug
good_example: -- Validation: SELECT SUM(amount) FROM source_table WHERE loaded_date = TODAY() -- different path to the same number
test_trigger: write a validation query for this report SQL

rule: Never define a KPI without specifying the inclusion and exclusion criteria
rationale: Every KPI implicitly excludes something. If exclusions are not written down, different implementations silently include different rows, and the metric forks.
bad_example: Active Users: count of users who logged in this month.
good_example: Active Users: count of distinct user_id values where login_date is within the current calendar month, status = 'active', and account_type NOT IN ('test','internal','demo'). Excludes: users created in the last 7 days.

rule: Never report on a column without checking its null rate first
rationale: A column that is 40% NULL will produce averages and sums that look plausible but represent only the non-null population. Dashboards built on such columns misrepresent the business.
bad_example: # Profile: just check row count and distinct values
test_trigger: profile this CSV file for reporting use

rule: Never infer join type from column name alone; verify with a cardinality check query
rationale: A column named customer_id in a fact table does not guarantee one row per customer. Lookups, bridge tables, and slowly changing dimensions can all produce unexpected fan-out.
bad_example: -- Assumed safe: customer_id looks like a FK so JOIN should be many-to-one
good_example: SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) as cnt FROM fact_orders GROUP BY customer_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 1 LIMIT 10 -- run before trusting cardinality
test_trigger: review this join between orders and customers

rule: Never let a DISTINCT mask a duplicate problem without investigating the source
rationale: DISTINCT hides duplication. It fixes the symptom (duplicate rows in the result) without exposing the cause (a fan-out join or a bad source). The duplicates may carry measure values that are being silently summed before dedup.
bad_example: SELECT DISTINCT customer_id, SUM(amount) FROM orders JOIN customers USING(customer_id) GROUP BY customer_id -- DISTINCT on a GROUP BY does nothing; SUM already ran on fan-out rows
good_example: -- First: find the duplicating join. Use ROW_NUMBER() to isolate which join stage fans out, fix the root cause, then remove DISTINCT.
test_trigger: this query has duplicates, add a distinct

rule: Never compare two datasets without confirming they share the same grain, period, and filter set
rationale: Reconciling a daily extract against a monthly report, or an active-only dataset against an all-records dataset, produces variance that is structural not a defect. Misidentifying this wastes investigation cycles.
bad_example: # Compare: source total vs warehouse total -- no grain check, no period filter alignment
good_example:
assert source_df['period'].nunique() == target_df['period'].nunique(), 'Period grain mismatch before reconciliation'
assert set(source_df['status'].unique()) == set(target_df['status'].unique()), 'Filter set mismatch'
test_trigger: reconcile these two files and explain the difference

rule: Never write requirements for a report without specifying the tie-break or sort behavior for equal values
rationale: Non-deterministic sort order makes top-N reports unstable across runs. Report consumers notice rank changes and file defects. The root cause is an underspecified requirement.
bad_example: Show the top 10 customers by revenue.
good_example: Show the top 10 customers by total revenue this month. Tie-break: alphabetical by customer_name ascending. If fewer than 10 customers exist, show all.
test_trigger: write a report spec for top 10 customers by revenue

rule: Never validate a report total using only row count; always reconcile a measure value
rationale: Row count can match perfectly while measure values are wrong. A join that fans out rows and then deduplicates can produce the correct count with inflated sums.
good_example:
assert len(report_df) == expected_rows
assert abs(report_df['revenue'].sum() - expected_revenue) < 0.01, f'Revenue mismatch: got {report_df["revenue"].sum()}, expected {expected_revenue}'
test_trigger: validate this report output before release

rule: Never treat a reconciliation difference of zero as proof of correctness
rationale: Offsetting errors can cancel out. A dataset with $1M overcounted in one segment and $1M undercounted in another reconciles to zero net difference while being wrong in both segments.
good_example:
if source_total == target_total:
    # Also check segment-level: a net zero can hide offsetting errors
    segment_diff = source_by_segment.merge(target_by_segment, on='segment')
    segment_diff['variance'] = segment_diff['source_amt'] - segment_diff['target_amt']
    assert segment_diff['variance'].abs().max() < threshold, 'Segment-level variance found despite net zero'
test_trigger: the reconciliation shows zero difference, we are good
